**Aerospace engineering** is the primary field of [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") concerned with the development of [aircraft](/wiki/Aircraft "Aircraft") and [spacecraft](/wiki/Spacecraft "Spacecraft").[[3]](#cite_note-3) It has two major and overlapping branches: [aeronautical](/wiki/Aeronautics "Aeronautics") engineering and [astronautical](/wiki/Astronautics "Astronautics") engineering. [Avionics](/wiki/Avionics "Avionics") engineering is similar, but deals with the [electronics](/wiki/Electronic_engineering "Electronic engineering") side of aerospace engineering.

"Aeronautical engineering" was the original term for the field. As flight technology advanced to include vehicles operating in [outer space](/wiki/Outer_space "Outer space"), the broader term "[aerospace](/wiki/Aerospace "Aerospace") engineering" has come into use.[[4]](#cite_note-britannica_Engineering-4) Aerospace engineering, particularly the astronautics branch, is often colloquially referred to as "rocket science".[[5]](#cite_note-SA-5)[[a]](#cite_note-fn1-7)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aerospace_engineering&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

Flight vehicles are subjected to demanding conditions such as those caused by changes in [atmospheric pressure](/wiki/Atmospheric_pressure "Atmospheric pressure") and [temperature](/wiki/Temperature "Temperature"), with [structural loads](/wiki/Structural_load "Structural load") applied upon vehicle components. Consequently, they are usually the products of various technological and engineering disciplines including [aerodynamics](/wiki/Aerodynamics "Aerodynamics"), [air propulsion](/wiki/Air_propulsion "Air propulsion"), [avionics](/wiki/Avionics "Avionics"), [materials science](/wiki/Materials_science "Materials science"), [structural analysis](/wiki/Structural_analysis "Structural analysis") and [manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing"). The interaction between these technologies is known as aerospace engineering. Because of the complexity and number of disciplines involved, aerospace engineering is carried out by teams of engineers, each having their own specialized area of expertise.[[7]](#cite_note-Princeton_review-8)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aerospace_engineering&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of aviation](/wiki/History_of_aviation "History of aviation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/First_flight2.jpg/250px-First_flight2.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_flight2.jpg)

[Orville and Wilbur Wright](/wiki/Orville_and_Wilbur_Wright "Orville and Wilbur Wright") flew the [Wright Flyer](/wiki/Wright_Flyer "Wright Flyer") in 1903 at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina.

The origin of aerospace engineering can be traced back to the aviation pioneers around the late 19th to early 20th centuries, although the work of Sir [George Cayley](/wiki/George_Cayley "George Cayley") dates from the last decade of the 18th to the mid-19th century. One of the most important people in the history of aeronautics[[8]](#cite_note-9) and a pioneer in aeronautical engineering,[[9]](#cite_note-10) Cayley is credited as the first person to separate the forces of [lift](/wiki/Lift_(force) "Lift (force)") and [drag](/wiki/Drag_(physics) "Drag (physics)"), which affect any atmospheric flight vehicle.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

Early knowledge of aeronautical engineering was largely empirical, with some concepts and skills imported from other branches of engineering.[[11]](#cite_note-americana-12) Some key elements, like [fluid dynamics](/wiki/Fluid_dynamics "Fluid dynamics"), were understood by 18th-century scientists.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

In December 1903, the [Wright Brothers](/wiki/Wright_brothers "Wright brothers") performed the first sustained, controlled flight of a powered, heavier-than-air aircraft, lasting 12 seconds. The 1910s saw the development of aeronautical engineering through the design of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") military aircraft.

### World War I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aerospace_engineering&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: World War I")]

In 1914, [Robert Goddard](/wiki/Robert_H._Goddard "Robert H. Goddard") was granted two U.S. patents for rockets using solid fuel, liquid fuel, multiple propellant charges, and multi-stage designs.[[13]](#cite_note-Astronautics_2024-14) This would set the stage for future applications in multi-stage propulsion systems for outer space.

On March 3, 1915, the U.S. Congress established the first aeronautical research administration, known as the [National Advisory Committee for Aeronautics](/wiki/National_Advisory_Committee_for_Aeronautics "National Advisory Committee for Aeronautics"), or NACA.[[14]](#cite_note-15) It was the first government-sponsored organization to support aviation research.[[13]](#cite_note-Astronautics_2024-14) Though intended as an advisory board upon inception, the Langley Aeronautical Laboratory became its first sponsored research and testing facility in 1920.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

Between World Wars I and II, great leaps were made in the field, accelerated by the advent of mainstream civil aviation. Notable airplanes of this era include the [Curtiss JN 4](/wiki/Curtiss_JN_4 "Curtiss JN 4"), [Farman F.60 Goliath](/wiki/Farman_F.60_Goliath "Farman F.60 Goliath"), and [Fokker Trimotor](/wiki/Fokker_Trimotor "Fokker Trimotor"). Notable military airplanes of this period include the [Mitsubishi A6M Zero](/wiki/Mitsubishi_A6M_Zero "Mitsubishi A6M Zero"), [Supermarine Spitfire](/wiki/Supermarine_Spitfire "Supermarine Spitfire") and [Messerschmitt Bf 109](/wiki/Messerschmitt_Bf_109 "Messerschmitt Bf 109") from Japan, United Kingdom, and Germany respectively. A significant development came with the first operational [Jet engine](/wiki/Jet_engine "Jet engine")-powered airplane, the [Messerschmitt Me 262](/wiki/Messerschmitt_Me_262 "Messerschmitt Me 262") which entered service in 1944 towards the end of the Second World War.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

The first definition of aerospace engineering appeared in February 1958,[[4]](#cite_note-britannica_Engineering-4) considering the Earth's atmosphere and outer space as a single realm, thereby encompassing both aircraft (*aero*) and spacecraft (*space*) under the newly coined term *aerospace*.

### Cold War

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aerospace_engineering&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Cold War")]

In response to the USSR launching the first satellite, [Sputnik](/wiki/Sputnik "Sputnik"), into space on October 4, 1957, U.S. aerospace engineers launched the [first American satellite](/wiki/Explorer_I "Explorer I") on January 31, 1958. The [National Aeronautics and Space Administration](/wiki/NASA "NASA") was founded in 1958 after the [Sputnik crisis](/wiki/Sputnik_crisis "Sputnik crisis"). In 1969, [Apollo 11](/wiki/Apollo_11 "Apollo 11"), the first human space mission to land on the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon"), took place. It saw three [astronauts](/wiki/Astronaut "Astronaut") enter orbit around the Moon, with two, [Neil Armstrong](/wiki/Neil_Armstrong "Neil Armstrong") and [Buzz Aldrin](/wiki/Buzz_Aldrin "Buzz Aldrin"), visiting the lunar surface. The third astronaut, [Michael Collins](/wiki/Michael_Collins_(astronaut) "Michael Collins (astronaut)"), stayed in orbit to rendezvous with Armstrong and Aldrin after their visit.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

[![A jet in flight](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/F-18F_after_launch_from_USS_Abraham_Lincoln_%28CVN-72%29.jpg/250px-F-18F_after_launch_from_USS_Abraham_Lincoln_%28CVN-72%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:F-18F_after_launch_from_USS_Abraham_Lincoln_(CVN-72).jpg)

A F/A-18F Super Hornet in flight, 2008

An important innovation came on January 30, 1970, when the [Boeing 747](/wiki/Boeing_747 "Boeing 747") made its first commercial flight from New York to London. This aircraft made history and became known as the "Jumbo Jet" or "Queen of the Skies"[[18]](#cite_note-19) due to its ability to hold up to 480 passengers.

### 1976: First passenger supersonic aircraft

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aerospace_engineering&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1976: First passenger supersonic aircraft")]

Another significant development came in 1976, with the development of the first passenger [supersonic](/wiki/Supersonic_transport "Supersonic transport") aircraft, the [Concorde](/wiki/Concorde "Concorde"). The development of this aircraft was agreed upon by the French and British on November 29, 1962.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

On December 21, 1988, the [Antonov An-225 *Mriya*](/wiki/Antonov_An-225_Mriya "Antonov An-225 Mriya") cargo aircraft commenced its first flight. It holds the records for the world's heaviest aircraft, heaviest airlifted cargo, and longest airlifted cargo of any aircraft in operational service.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

On October 25, 2007, the [Airbus A380](/wiki/Airbus_A380 "Airbus A380") made its maiden commercial flight from Singapore to Sydney, Australia. This aircraft was the first passenger plane to surpass the [Boeing 747](/wiki/Boeing_747 "Boeing 747") in terms of passenger capacity, with a maximum of 853. Though development of this aircraft began in 1988 as a competitor to the 747, the A380 made its first test flight in April 2005.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

See also: [List of aerospace engineering topics](/wiki/List_of_aerospace_engineering_topics "List of aerospace engineering topics")

Some of the elements of aerospace engineering are:[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-gruntman-24)

* [Radar cross-section](/wiki/Radar_cross-section "Radar cross-section") – the study of vehicle signature apparent to [remote sensing](/wiki/Remote_sensing "Remote sensing") by [radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar").
* [Fluid mechanics](/wiki/Fluid_mechanics "Fluid mechanics") – the study of [fluid flow](/wiki/Fluid_flow "Fluid flow") around objects. Specifically [aerodynamics](/wiki/Aerodynamics "Aerodynamics") concerning the flow of air over bodies such as [wings](/wiki/Wing "Wing") or through objects such as [wind tunnels](/wiki/Wind_tunnel "Wind tunnel") (see also [lift](/wiki/Lift_(force) "Lift (force)") and [aeronautics](/wiki/Aeronautics "Aeronautics")).
* [Astrodynamics](/wiki/Astrodynamics "Astrodynamics") – the study of [orbital mechanics](/wiki/Orbital_mechanics "Orbital mechanics") including prediction of orbital elements when given a select few variables. While few schools in the United States teach this at the undergraduate level, several have graduate programs covering this topic (usually in conjunction with the Physics department of said college or university).
* [Statics](/wiki/Statics "Statics") and [Dynamics](/wiki/Dynamics_(physics) "Dynamics (physics)") (engineering mechanics) – the study of movement, forces, moments in mechanical systems.
* [Mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics") – in particular, [calculus](/wiki/Calculus "Calculus"), [differential equations](/wiki/Differential_equations "Differential equations"), and [linear algebra](/wiki/Linear_algebra "Linear algebra").
* [Electrotechnology](/wiki/Electrotechnology "Electrotechnology") – the study of [electronics](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics") within engineering.
* [Propulsion](/wiki/Propulsion "Propulsion") – the energy to move a vehicle through the air (or in outer space) is provided by [internal combustion engines](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine "Internal combustion engine"), [jet engines](/wiki/Jet_engine "Jet engine") and [turbomachinery](/wiki/Turbomachinery "Turbomachinery"), or [rockets](/wiki/Rocket "Rocket") (see also [propeller](/wiki/Propeller_(aircraft) "Propeller (aircraft)") and [spacecraft propulsion](/wiki/Spacecraft_propulsion "Spacecraft propulsion")). A more recent addition to this module is [electric propulsion](/wiki/Electric_propulsion "Electric propulsion") and [ion propulsion](/wiki/Ion_propulsion "Ion propulsion").
* [Control engineering](/wiki/Control_engineering "Control engineering") – the study of [mathematical modeling](/wiki/Mathematical_modeling "Mathematical modeling") of the [dynamic](/wiki/Flight_dynamics "Flight dynamics") behavior of systems and designing them, usually using feedback signals, so that their dynamic behavior is desirable (stable, without large excursions, with minimum error). This applies to the dynamic behavior of aircraft, spacecraft, propulsion systems, and subsystems that exist on aerospace vehicles.
* [Aircraft structures](/wiki/Aircraft_structures "Aircraft structures") – design of the physical configuration of the craft to withstand the forces encountered during flight. Aerospace engineering aims to keep structures lightweight and low-cost while maintaining structural integrity.[[24]](#cite_note-25)
* [Materials science](/wiki/Materials_science "Materials science") – related to structures, aerospace engineering also studies the materials of which the aerospace structures are to be built. New materials with very specific properties are invented, or existing ones are modified to improve their performance.
* [Solid mechanics](/wiki/Solid_mechanics "Solid mechanics") – Closely related to material science is solid mechanics which deals with stress and strain analysis of the components of the vehicle. Nowadays there are several Finite Element programs such as MSC Patran/Nastran which aid engineers in the analytical process.
* [Aeroelasticity](/wiki/Aeroelasticity "Aeroelasticity") – the interaction of aerodynamic forces and structural flexibility, potentially causing [flutter](/wiki/Aeroelastic_flutter "Aeroelastic flutter"), divergence, etc.
* [Avionics](/wiki/Avionics "Avionics") – the design and programming of [computer systems](/wiki/Computer_systems "Computer systems") on board an aircraft or spacecraft and the [simulation](/wiki/Simulation "Simulation") of systems.
* [Software](/wiki/Software "Software") – the specification, design, development, test, and implementation of [computer software](/wiki/Computer_software "Computer software") for aerospace applications, including [flight software](/wiki/Flight_software "Flight software"), [ground control](/wiki/Mission_control_center "Mission control center") software, test & evaluation software, etc.
* [Risk and reliability](/wiki/Reliability_engineering "Reliability engineering") – the study of risk and reliability assessment techniques and the mathematics involved in the quantitative methods.
* [Noise control](/wiki/Noise_control "Noise control") – the study of the mechanics of sound transfer.
* [Aeroacoustics](/wiki/Aeroacoustics "Aeroacoustics") – the study of noise generation via either turbulent fluid motion or aerodynamic forces interacting with surfaces.
* [Flight testing](/wiki/Flight_testing "Flight testing") – designing and executing flight test programs in order to gather and analyze performance and [handling qualities](/wiki/Handling_qualities "Handling qualities") data in order to determine if an aircraft meets its design and performance goals and certification requirements.

The basis of most of these elements lies in theoretical [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"), such as [fluid dynamics](/wiki/Fluid_dynamics "Fluid dynamics") for aerodynamics or the [equations of motion](/wiki/Equations_of_motion "Equations of motion") for [flight dynamics](/wiki/Flight_dynamics "Flight dynamics"). There is also a large [empirical](/wiki/Empiricism "Empiricism") component. Historically, this empirical component was derived from testing of scale models and prototypes, either in [wind tunnels](/wiki/Wind_tunnel "Wind tunnel") or in the free atmosphere. More recently, advances in [computing](/wiki/Computing "Computing") have enabled the use of [computational fluid dynamics](/wiki/Computational_fluid_dynamics "Computational fluid dynamics") to simulate the behavior of the fluid, reducing time and expense spent on wind-tunnel testing. Those studying hydrodynamics or [hydroacoustics](/wiki/Hydroacoustics "Hydroacoustics") often obtain degrees in aerospace engineering.

Additionally, aerospace engineering addresses the integration of all components that constitute an aerospace vehicle (subsystems including power, [aerospace bearings](/wiki/Aerospace_bearing "Aerospace bearing"), communications, [thermal control](/wiki/Temperature_control "Temperature control"), [life support system](/wiki/Life_support_system "Life support system"), etc.) and its life cycle (design, temperature, pressure, [radiation](/wiki/Radiation "Radiation"), [velocity](/wiki/Velocity "Velocity"), [lifetime](/wiki/Fatigue_(material) "Fatigue (material)")).

## Degree programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aerospace_engineering&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Degree programs")]

Main article: [List of aerospace engineering schools](/wiki/List_of_aerospace_engineering_schools "List of aerospace engineering schools")

Aerospace engineering may be studied at the advanced diploma, [bachelor's](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree"), [master's](/wiki/Master%27s_degree "Master's degree"), and [Ph.D.](/wiki/Doctor_of_Philosophy "Doctor of Philosophy") levels in aerospace engineering departments at many universities, and in [mechanical engineering](/wiki/Mechanical_engineering "Mechanical engineering") departments at others. A few departments offer degrees in space-focused astronautical engineering. Some institutions differentiate between aeronautical and astronautical engineering. Graduate degrees are offered in advanced or specialty areas for the aerospace industry.

A background in chemistry, physics, computer science and mathematics is important for students pursuing an aerospace engineering degree.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aerospace_engineering&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The term "[rocket scientist](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/rocket_scientist "wikt:rocket scientist")" is sometimes used to describe a person of great [intelligence](/wiki/Intelligence "Intelligence") since rocket science is seen as a practice requiring great mental ability, especially technically and mathematically. The term is used ironically in the expression "It's not rocket science" to indicate that a task is simple.[[26]](#cite_note-27) Strictly speaking, the use of "science" in "rocket science" is a misnomer since science is about understanding the origins, nature, and behavior of the universe; engineering is about using scientific and engineering principles to solve problems and develop new technology.[[5]](#cite_note-SA-5)[[6]](#cite_note-IEEE-6) The more [etymologically](/wiki/Etymology "Etymology") correct version of this phrase would be "rocket engineer". However, "science" and "engineering" are often misused as synonyms.[[5]](#cite_note-SA-5)[[6]](#cite_note-IEEE-6)[[27]](#cite_note-28)
